summary.one_line: The operator's final zoom edit revealed a strong tiered system; the main miss was underestimating frequency and section-level consistency.

artifacts_reviewed: 16 extracted frames from operator zoom points

followed: performed frame-by-frame analysis on a sample of operator zooms
translated observations into reusable zoom design rules

issues.title: Suggested zoom frequency was far too low
issues.description: The operator used roughly twice as many zooms, showing that this format benefits from frequent visual guidance.
issues.operator_takeaway: Prefer more frequent, gentler zooms over sparse aggressive ones.

issues.title: Section-level consistency matters more than per-zoom uniqueness
issues.description: The operator reused scale ranges and anchor patterns within topics instead of treating every zoom as a unique event.
issues.operator_takeaway: Plan zooms by section first, then by moment.

issues.title: Overhang is a primary continuity tool
issues.description: Most operator zooms extended across adjacent clips, smoothing jump cuts and making thoughts feel continuous.
issues.operator_takeaway: Think in phrases rather than clip boundaries.

recommendations.action: Increase target zoom density to about 3–4 per minute.
Make section-based zoom planning and scale consistency first-class guidance.
Treat overhang as a default continuity technique rather than a special case.
Encode webcam head-room constraints and matched-pair/scroll-follow patterns explicitly in future planning heuristics.

feedback_hooks.question: Does the 3–4/min target generalize across future layouts and video formats?
feedback_hooks.how_to_compare: Track operator zoom counts and densities across future diff evals.

feedback_hooks.question: Do the tier boundaries stay stable across different recording layouts?
feedback_hooks.how_to_compare: Compare future section-scale clusters in sidecars.
